channel.title: Dataviz on jgoldfar's home on the web
channel.description: Recent content in Dataviz on jgoldfar's home on the web

item.title: Visualizing Attendance Data using Idiomatic Julia
item.description: In a previous post I shared a quick-and-dirty code I wrote as part of a larger script that generates a bunch of reports. This script dates back to when local environments/dependencies didn’t have quite as nice a story as Julia v1.0 does, so I made sure to only use standard library packages. But to make a fair comparison with the other codes in R and Python (in particular, to see how semantically clear one could make the code) one should use high-quality data analysis packages provided by the Julia ecosystem!

item.title: Visualizing Attendance Data
item.description: In this post I’m going to share some simple data munging and visualization techniques I use to analyze attendance data recorded from a card swipe machine in our tutoring lab, which I use to make sure we use our office hours (a human time resource!) as effectively as possible, and report that internally. I’ve had this running on a regular basis since I was first asked to report this data some time back; in the past, Excel was used to prepare these charts, but it had to be done basically entirely “by hand” each time.

item.title: Installing Jupyter with IJulia and IRkernel
item.description: In this post, I’ll share the commands I followed to install JupyterLab using Miniconda on an OSX platform, as part of the preparation I’m doing for our open source data analysis course; it’s important to me that everyone is able to reproduce the examples on their own machine and experiment for themselves. Update The installation of R, Julia, etc. are now automated for MacOS using Ansible; see this post. If you’re following those directions, after installing Ansible and the corresponding playbook, simply run
